thread_title: Observational Learning, Mirror Neurons & Expert Modeling
seeneyu_relevance: Before users record themselves, they watch Hollywood actors — expert performers of non-verbal communication. This is structured observational learning. The neurological basis (mirror neurons, motor system pre-activation) explains why watching skilled models before practicing is more effective than practicing without observation.
hypothesis: Watching skilled performers execute a target behavior activates the observer's motor system, creating a neural template that improves subsequent physical reproduction of the behavior. This explains why Watch comes before Record in seeneyu's loop.
key_finding: Observational learning activates the same neural motor circuits as physical practice. Social information 'feeds into circuits involved in conventional direct learning.' Watching expert models before practicing produces better outcomes than practice alone.
evidence_grade_summary: B/C — Strong neuroscientific evidence for mirror neuron activation during observation; moderate evidence for transfer to behavioral performance in humans.

sources.title: Social Learning Theory
sources.key_finding: Learning occurs by observing others — no direct experience required. Four processes: Attention → Retention → Motor Reproduction → Motivation. Expert modeling activates all four simultaneously. Performance improves faster when learners first observe skilled models.
sources.seeneyu_application: seeneyu's 'Watch' phase is a precisely structured Bandura observational learning session: users attend to a specific skill, retain via Observation Guide annotations, then reproduce via recording.
sources.quote: From observing others, one forms an idea of how new behaviors are performed, and on later occasions this coded information serves as a guide for action.

sources.title: The mirror-neuron system
sources.key_finding: Mirror neurons in the premotor cortex fire both during action execution and during observation of the same action. This 'action observation network' creates a direct link between watching and doing — observation alone activates motor preparation.
sources.seeneyu_application: When users watch an Iron Man scene showing confident open posture, their premotor cortex fires as if they were performing the posture. This neurological priming makes subsequent practice (recording) more accurate.
sources.quote: Mirror neurons discharge during both the execution of motor acts and during the observation of similar acts performed by other individuals.

sources.title: Mirror neurons: From origin to function
sources.key_finding: Mirror neurons develop through associative learning — they are not innate. They form by pairing sensory observations with self-produced actions. This means mirror neuron strength for a skill can be trained by repeatedly pairing watching expert models with personal practice.
sources.seeneyu_application: Each Watch → Record cycle strengthens the user's mirror neuron associations for target skills. The pairing is the mechanism. This directly validates seeneyu's loop as neurologically optimal.
sources.limitation: Direct measurement of mirror neurons in humans requires invasive methods; most human evidence is from fMRI showing mirror-system homologs rather than single-cell recording.

sources.title: Motor learning by observation: Evidence from a serial reaction time task
sources.journal: Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A
sources.key_finding: Motor learning from observation produces the same implicit sequence learning effects as physical practice. Watching a skilled performer activates motor programs — observation is motor practice by another means.
sources.seeneyu_application: The 'Watch' phase is not passive content consumption — it is motor practice. Users are literally building motor memory by watching skilled performers.

sources.title: Motor learning by observing
sources.key_finding: Controlled experiment: subjects who watched a skilled performer adapted their motor system as if they had physically practiced. Observation-based motor adaptation was statistically indistinguishable from practice-based adaptation in the early trials.
sources.seeneyu_application: Empirical confirmation that watching transfers motor skill. The Watch phase is doing real neuroscientific work — not just inspiration.
sources.quote: Observation of another person's movements can cause adaptation of the observer's own motor system.

sources.title: Neural circuits underlying imitation learning of hand actions: An event-related fMRI study
sources.key_finding: fMRI shows that imitation learning activates a distinct neural circuit (STS → inferior frontal gyrus → primary motor cortex). This circuit is specifically activated when watching an expert model for the purpose of imitation — more strongly than passive observation.
sources.seeneyu_application: The instruction to 'watch this scene to mimic it' activates a different, stronger neural circuit than passive watching. The framing of watching-to-mimic is neurologically superior to just watching. seeneyu's Observation Guide creates exactly this intentional watching context.
